Question: Eric paid $$\text{$16.64}$$ for stamps so he could mail thank you notes for his wedding gifts. The number of $$\text{49-cent}$$ stamps was eight more than twice the number of $$\text{8-cent}$$ stamps. How many $$\text{49-cent}$$ stamps and how many $$\text{8-cent}$$ stamps did Eric buy?

  y = number of 8-cent stamps

   0.49x + 0.08y = 16.64

2. Relationship between the numbers  
   x = 2y + 8     (eight more than twice the number of 8-cent stamps)

Substitute x from (2) into (1):

0.49(2y + 8) + 0.08y = 16.64  
0.98y + 3.92 + 0.08y = 16.64  
1.06y + 3.92 = 16.64  
1.06y = 16.64 – 3.92  
1.06y = 12.72  
y = 12

x = 2y + 8 = 2(12) + 8 = 32

Eric bought 32 of the 49-cent stamps and 12 of the 8-cent stamps.

Question: Kailee paid $$\text{$14.84}$$ for stamps. The number of $$\text{49-cent}$$ stamps was four less than three times the number of $$\text{21-cent}$$ stamps. How many $$\text{49-cent}$$ stamps and how many $$\text{21-cent}$$ stamps did Kailee buy?

y = number of 49-cent stamps  

1. Translate the relationship between the numbers of stamps:

3. Substitute y from (1) into the money equation:  
   21x + 49(3x – 4) = 1484  
   21x + 147x – 196 = 1484  
   168x – 196 = 1484  
   168x = 1484 + 196  
   168x = 1680  
   x = 10

   y = 3x – 4 = 3(10) – 4 = 26

Therefore, Kailee bought
